title: State-building: The Persian Empire
description: Let's analyze some historical documents that pertain to the Persian Empire, its great cities, and its expansion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read this passage carefully:

> "According to the Roman geographer Strabo of Amasia, the palace of Pasargadae was built on the site where king Cyrus (r.559-530) defeated the leader of the Medes, Astyages, in 550 BCE (Strabo, Geography, 15.3.8).  ...

> If he did not build the palace on this site because of a military victory, there may have been other reasons: the place is beautifully situated in the center of a fertile plain, on all sides surrounded by mountains. It is essentially a valley that was filled by sediments from the river Pulvar. …"

From Pasargardae

**What are some possible reasons that Cyrus chose the location of Pasargadae?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The site didn't have significance for the Medes until Astyages was defeated there.
Pasargadae wasn't in the geographical center of the empire.

options.explanation: This excerpt comes from the [Ancient History Encyclopedia](http://www.ancient.eu/Pasargadae/).

choices.content: He wished to commemorate his victory over Astyages
He desired an economically attractive location for his new capital
Pasargadae was to be in the geographical center of his empire
Cyrus wanted to build over a site that had significance to the Medes

assessment_items.question_content:
> "The great city of Persepolis was built in terraces up from the river Pulvar to rise on a larger terrace of over 125,000 square feet, partly cut out of the Mountain Kuh-e Rahmet. To create the level terrace, large depressions were filled with soil and heavy rocks which were then fastened together with metal clips; upon this ground the first palace at Persepolis slowly grew. ….

> Limestone was the main building material used in Persepolis. After natural rock had been leveled and the depressions filled in, tunnels for sewage were dug underground through the rock. A large elevated cistern was carved at the eastern foot of the mountain to catch rainwater for drinking and bathing. ...

> The terraced plan of the site around the palace walls enabled the Persians to easily defend any section of the front. The ancient historian Diodorus Siculus recorded that Persepolis had three walls with ramparts, all with fortified towers, always manned. The first wall was over seven feet tall, the second, fourteen feet, and the third wall, surrounding all four sides, was thirty feet high.  …

> Because of its remote location in the mountains, however, travel to Persepolis was almost impossible during the rainy season of the Persian winter when paths turned to mud and so the city was used mainly in the spring and summer warmer seasons."


![Ruins of the Gate of All Nations, Persepolis](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5d0540a2bc52f3beb8d14d2b2983b577.jpg)
[Ruins of the Gate of All Nations, Persepolis](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=45347465)
Image credit: Wikimedia user  Alborzagros, CC BY-SA 3.0, 


**Darius built Persepolis in the location he did because**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Persepolis was not easy to access: it was cut out of a mountain and surrounded by fortified walls; when the rainy season came, the paths to Persepolis turned to mud.
Pasargadae was built on the site of a military victory by Cyrus.  Darius built Persepolis somewhere else.

choices.content: it was hard to access, and thus easier to defend
he won a military victory at this location

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, review this map of Persian expansion:

![This gif shows the expansion of the Achaemenid empire over time, followed by its contraction and decline following the invasion by Alexander of Macedon. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8dedbac8ad4448b704fe0a3bfd2d9e0f.gif)
Image credit: Wikimedia Commons user [Ali Zifan](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AAchaemenid_Empire.gif)

This gif shows the expansion of the Achaemenid empire over time, followed by its contraction and decline following the invasion by Alexander of Macedon. 

The changes to the Persian empire shown in the maps between 553 and 518 BCE were largely the result of 
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The spread of Zoroastrianism may have been a consequence of imperial conquest, but it didn't cause the borders of Persia to expand.
The states that joined the Persian empire did not do so willingly.

choices.content: Persians immigrating and populating more territory
Persian conquests that established client states and satrapies
other states voluntarily joining the Persian empire
the spread of Zoroastrianism leading more people to identify as Persians

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, review this map of Persian expansion:

![This gif shows the expansion of the Achaemenid empire over time, followed by its contraction and decline following the invasion by Alexander of Macedon. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8dedbac8ad4448b704fe0a3bfd2d9e0f.gif)
Image credit: Wikimedia Commons user [Ali Zifan](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AAchaemenid_Empire.gif).

This gif shows the expansion of the Achaemenid empire over time, followed by its contraction and decline following the invasion by Alexander of Macedon. 

Which of the following is a defensible historical claim that would connect the construction of Persepolis, which began around 519 BCE, to imperial expansion? 
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The building of Persepolis did not cause the Persian empire to shrink.
It's unclear from the map that the building of Persepolis made the Persians appear more powerful.

choices.content: Persepolis being built was unrelated to the expansion of the Persian empire
As Persian control expanded, the empire had access to more resources, which Darius was then able to use to construct his new capital
The construction of a new capital at Persepolis made the Persians appear more powerful, allowing them to conquer more territory
Building Persepolis caused the Persian empire to shrink, as more resources were routed away from the frontiers
